title: Reduce official image size
body:
Current image size is 728MB.

1. Switch to fedora-minimal. Requires switching to dnf5. shadow-utils is
no longer included in the base image, so the bits about it are renmoved.
728MB -> 650MB.
2. Don't update unrelated packages. 650MB -> 598MB.

Ultimately the image is still very large, which is driven by the veritable constellation of dependencies buildah installs.  Installing `buildah` in fedora-minimal gives you this plan:

There's a lot of confusing stuff in there, and we can't do anything to fix that here.

#### How to verify it

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
See #5321. I won't say it fixes the issue. The image is still huge.

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

```release-note
Changed the base image of the official buidah image to fedora-minimal to reduce its size.
```

comments.body:
Compare to debian's plan for installing buildah (in `debian:testing-slim`):
```
The following additional packages will be installed:
  aardvark-dns adduser containernetworking-plugins crun dirmngr dmsetup
  fuse-overlayfs fuse3 gnupg gnupg-l10n gnupg-utils golang-github-containers-common
  golang-github-containers-image gpg gpg-agent gpg-wks-client gpg-wks-server gpgconf
  gpgsm iptables libassuan0 libbsd0 libdevmapper1.02.1 libedit2 libfuse3-3
  libgpgme11 libgpm2 libip4tc2 libip6tc2 libjansson4 libksba8 libldap-2.5-0
  libldap-common libmnl0 libncursesw6 libnetfilter-conntrack3 libnfnetlink0
  libnftables1 libnftnl11 libnpth0 libreadline8 libsasl2-2 libsasl2-modules
  libsasl2-modules-db libsqlite3-0 libsubid4 libxtables12 libyajl2 netavark netbase
  nftables pinentry-curses readline-common uidmap
Suggested packages:
  liblocale-gettext-perl perl cron quota containers-storage libwasmedge0
  dbus-user-session libpam-systemd pinentry-gnome3 tor parcimonie xloadimage
  scdaemon firewalld kmod gpm libsasl2-modules-gssapi-mit
  | libsasl2-modules-gssapi-heimdal libsasl2-modules-ldap libsasl2-modules-otp
  libsasl2-modules-sql pinentry-doc readline-doc
Recommended packages:
  gnupg
The following NEW packages will be installed:
  aardvark-dns adduser buildah containernetworking-plugins crun dirmngr dmsetup
  fuse-overlayfs fuse3 gnupg gnupg-l10n gnupg-utils golang-github-containers-common
  golang-github-containers-image gpg gpg-agent gpg-wks-client gpg-wks-server gpgconf
  gpgsm iptables libassuan0 libbsd0 libdevmapper1.02.1 libedit2 libfuse3-3
  libgpgme11 libgpm2 libip4tc2 libip6tc2 libjansson4 libksba8 libldap-2.5-0
  libldap-common libmnl0 libncursesw6 libnetfilter-conntrack3 libnfnetlink0
  libnftables1 libnftnl11 libnpth0 libreadline8 libsasl2-2 libsasl2-modules
  libsasl2-modules-db libsqlite3-0 libsubid4 libxtables12 libyajl2 netavark netbase
  nftables pinentry-curses readline-common uidmap
0 upgraded, 55 newly installed, 0 to remove and 8 not upgraded.
Need to get 26.5 MB of archives.
After this operation, 117 MB of additional disk space will be used.
```
Notably no `python`, no `systemd`, among many others. Though the lack of `qemu` is certainly cheating.

I was able to build this buildah image using only `apt -y install buildah ca-certificates` in that slim image.

Here's the resulting image size:
```
λ cat Dockerfile
FROM debian:testing-slim
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y buildah ca-certificates && apt-get clean
λ docker image build . -t debian-buildah
# . . .
λ docker image ls
REPOSITORY                                  TAG            IMAGE ID       CREATED          SIZE
debian-buildah                              latest         d69028ba4101   19 seconds ago   237MB
```

> There's a lot of confusing stuff in there, and we can't do anything to fix that here.

Just kidding, the RPM is defined here 🌞.  But I think futzing with it is beyond me.
https://github.com/containers/buildah/blob/main/rpm/buildah.spec

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *giuseppe*, *isker*, *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fbuildah). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/buildah/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [giuseppe,rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@cevich heads up, PTAL when you get a chance.

-sigh- it's already merged :cry:  I was going to propose, since the Debian version is so drastically smaller, maybe we could switch to it :smile:  Or...perhaps we could force-remove the "extra" qemu bits (are they actually useful in an app container image?)

With Debian, the challenge is in getting a particular version installed. I assume you don’t want to be tied to their packaging timelines when cutting these docker images.

Qemu is presumably needed in this image for cross platform builds, just as it is general.

Yeah, though for the stable flavor, maybe it wouldn't be too bad?  But in general I understand, and I believe Fedora will move forward faster.

Qemu in the container image can't work for cross-platform builds.  The host kernel would need to be told to use them explicitly.  This would be problematic on a system that's juggling more than one image tag.  In that case, "who knows" what qemu is really being used or what container storage is safe to prune?

Maybe the qemu-user-static dependency can be fixed (made optional?) on the packaging side?

If the project is committing to publishing a tagged image for every release it cuts (I don’t actually know that this is true, to be clear), then I think Debian is not an option. I don’t think that there’s a guarantee that any particular version will land in Debian at all, let alone when it will land.

Re: qemu, got it. We’ve already gone beyond my understanding then 🌞. Though I will note that buildkit images do include it. Given your explanation, I’m not sure why.

But it might make even more sense to focus on things like systemd, python3-libs, a cracked password database, etc, over qemu.  I tried and failed to figure out how to get an explanation from dnf as to why a particular package is included in that installation plan in the PR description.

> But it might make even more sense to focus on things like systemd, python3-libs, a cracked password database, etc, over

Though I agree, I think removing qemu might be **much** lower hanging fruit.  Python is well entrenched with `dnf` and can't be easily swapped to microdnf w/o affecting people (see #5349).
